claim: S1 steel with ≥6 wt.% Al exhibited the fastest bainitic transformation at 350 °C with a transformation time of 1600 s.
evidence_text: In steels with ≥6 % wt.% Al, the fastest transformation was determined for S1 at 350 °C, with a transformation time 1600 s.

claim: Yield strength (Rp0.2) trends generally matched the UTS, except for S3 at low austempering temperature.
evidence_text: Yield strength (Rp0.2) trends generally matched the UTS, except for S3 at low austempering temperature.

claim: Microstructural precipitate analysis confirmed the primary formation of (Fe,M)xCy and TiMoC; MatCalc simulations indicated primary precipitation above austempering temperatures at the austenitizing and cooling stages.
evidence_text: Microstructural precipitate analysis confirmed the primary formation of (Fe,M)xCy and TiMoC. MatCalc simulations supported these findings, indicating primary precipitation above austempering temperatures at the austenitizing and cooling stages.

claim: Steels S1, S2, S3, and S4 were designed with Al levels of 5.8–8 wt.%, Mn limited to 1 wt.%, high C content of 0.9–1.0 wt.% to achieve full austenitization without polygonal ferrite and maintain Ms temperature around 300 °C; Si was kept at 0.3 wt.% to suppress carbide precipitation; Mo and B were added for hardenability.
evidence_text: For steels S1, S2, S3, and S4, incorporating Al at levels of 5.8–8 wt.% and limiting Mn content to 1 wt.% helps stabilize the ferrite phase... a high C content of 0.9–1.0 wt.% is required to achieve full austenitization without polygonal ferrite phases and to maintain a Ms temperature around 300 °C. Si is added to suppress the carbide precipitation, but its content is kept low at 0.3 wt.%... Mo is included to enhance hardenability, while micro-alloying with B is utilized to further improve hardenability only for S1, S2, S3, and S4

claim: S4 steel was designed with slightly lower Al content to suppress polygonal ferrite, reduced C to accelerate bainitic transformation and mitigate brittleness, Ti microalloying to enhance bainitic transformation and mechanical properties, and 0.78% Ni to prevent δ-ferrite formation.
evidence_text: In Steel S4, a slightly lower Al content was utilized to suppress αp formation, while a reduced C content was employed to accelerate the bainitic transformation and mitigate brittleness. Additionally, Ti microalloying was applied... 0.78 % Ni was added to the composition.

claim: S5 alloy contains 0.6% C and 3.92% Al with 0.78% Ni addition to promote an αp-free microstructure without compromising bainitic transformation kinetics or strength.
evidence_text: Alloy S5 is designed with a lower alloying concept. This alloy contains 0.6 % C and 3.92 % Al. The addition of Ni promotes an αp free microstructure without compromising the bainitic transformation kinetics or reducing the resulting strength... Ni content was minimized to 0.78 %

claim: In steels S1, S2, S3, and S4, Al is incorporated at 5.8–8 wt.% and Mn is limited to 1 wt.% to stabilize ferrite and accelerate transformation, while a high C content of 0.9–1.0 wt.% is required to achieve full austenitization and maintain an Ms temperature around 300 °C.
evidence_text: For steels S1, S2, S3, and S4, incorporating Al at levels of 5.8–8 wt.% and limiting Mn content to 1 wt.% helps stabilize the ferrite phase, thereby accelerating the transformation process. … To address this, a high C content of 0.9–1.0 wt.% is required to achieve full austenitization without polygonal ferrite … and to maintain a Ms temperature around 300 °C.

claim: Alloy S5 employs a lower-alloying concept with 0.6 wt.% C and 3.92 wt.% Al, and the addition of 0.78 wt.% Ni promotes an αp-free microstructure without compromising bainitic transformation kinetics or strength.
evidence_text: Alloy S5 is designed with a lower alloying concept. This alloy contains 0.6 % C and 3.92 % Al. The addition of Ni promotes an αp free microstructure without compromising the bainitic transformation kinetics or reducing the resulting strength … Ni content was minimized to 0.78 % due to its high cost and austenite stabilization effect in the steel S4 and S5.

claim: The cast steel blocks were homogenized at 1100 °C for 24 h under inert argon protection to promote uniform composition and microstructure.
evidence_text: Following casting, the steel undergoes homogenization at 1100 °C for 24 h with inert argon protection, promoting uniform composition and microstructure throughout the steel blocks.

claim: Austenitizing temperatures TA were selected above calculated and measured values to dissolve all carbides and avoid αp; Ms was determined by dilatometric analysis using a cooling rate of 100 K/s from TA and a tangent-intersection analysis of the RLC curves.
evidence_text: The applied austenitizing temperatures were selected above the calculated and measured values, to ensure a microstructure as free as possible from αp and to dissolve all possible carbides. The martensite start temperature (Ms) was determined using dilatometric analysis by rapid cooling at a rate of 100 K/s from the corresponding applied TA. Ms on cooling was obtained by a tangent-intersection analysis of the dilatometric RLC curves.

claim: Additional heat treatments at 1250 °C were applied to steel S4 to facilitate maximum dissolution of MX C-type precipitates and release the impingement effect.
evidence_text: Additionally, to facilitate the maximum dissolution of MX C-type precipitates and release the impingement effect, additional heat treatments at 1250 °C were conducted on S4 steel.

claim: The bainitic transformation finish time Bt is defined as the point where the transformation rate decreases to a negligible level, corresponding to the onset of the plateau in the RLC curve (Fig. 1(b)).
evidence_text: The transformation finish time (Bt) was defined as the point where the transformation rate decreased to a negligible level, corresponding to the onset of the plateau in the RLC curve. … This corresponds to the beginning of the plateau region in the relative length change (RLC (%)) curve shown in Fig. 1 (b)).

claim: Quantitative image analysis of primary αp volume fraction and spatial distribution was performed only for steel S3 due to its higher polygonal ferrite content, using light-optical micrographs at 200× magnification from five distinct regions.
evidence_text: Quantitative image analysis of the primary αp volume fraction and spatial distribution was performed only for steel S3, selected due to its higher polygonal ferrite content, using ImageJ … on light-optical micrographs (200 ×) acquired from five distinct regions.

claim: For bainite thickness determination, 100 measurements were made from 10 SEM images per specimen (10 measurements per image), and the true lamellar thickness Lt(t) was obtained from the mean linear intercept Lt using the stereological correction Lt(t)=2Lt/π.
evidence_text: For each condition, 100 measurements were conducted from 10 SEM images per specimen, with 10 measurements taken per image. The true lamellar thickness, Lt(t), was calculated by first measuring the mean linear intercept, Lt, perpendicular to the length of the bainitic lamellae. … a stereological correction was applied using the relationship Lt(t)=2Lt/π.

claim: The retained austenite volume fraction Vγ was determined by XRD Rietveld refinement according to ASTM E975-13, applying a correction for the carbide volume fraction Vc using Eq. (1): Vγ=(1-Vc)(Iγ/Rγ)/[(Iα/Rα)+(Iγ/Rγ)], with the overall method summarized in Fig. 2.
evidence_text: The volume fraction of retained austenite (Vγ) … were determined using the Rietveld refinement method, … following the guidelines of ASTM E975-13. … Vγ = (1-Vc)(Iγ/Rγ)/[(Iα/Rα)+(Iγ/Rγ)] (Eq.1). … The applied method for Vγ determination is summarized in Fig. 2.

claim: κ-carbides were treated as fixed phase fractions in precipitation simulations because they form via spinodal decomposition, which is incompatible with classical nucleation theory.
evidence_text: κ-carbides were considered as fixed phase fractions due to the consideration of that κ-carbides form with the spinodal decomposition, hence inappropriate for classical nucleation theory.

claim: Approximate austenitizing temperatures (TA) were determined from differentiated RLC–Temperature curves as 1062 °C for S1, 1031 °C for S2, no unique TA for S3 due to a broadened transition, 1067 °C for S4, and ~900 °C for S5, as shown in Fig. 3.
evidence_text: Using this criterion, approximate T_A values were obtained as 1062 °C (S1), 1031 ◦C (S2), a broadened transition without a unique T_A for S3, 1067 °C (S4), and ∼900 °C (S5) as shown in Fig. 3.

claim: MatCalc simulations predicted ferrite-free austenitizing temperatures of 991 °C (S1), 929 °C (S2), 1177 °C (S3), 1016 °C (S4), and 986 °C (S5), and complete dissolution of MC-type (Fe,M)xCy carbides at 1103–1107 °C for S1–S2 and near 1134 °C for S4.
evidence_text: The simulations indicated ferrite-free TA values of 991 ◦C (S1), 929 ◦C (S2), 1177 ◦C (S3), 1016 ◦C (S4), and 986 ◦C (S5) and predicted complete dissolution of MC-type (Fe, M)xCy carbides around 1103-1107 ◦C for S1–S2 and near 1134 ◦C for S4.

claim: Light optical microscopy at 1050 °C revealed extensive polygonal ferrite (αp) in S3 with an area fraction of ~14.6%, and αp persisted even at 1220 °C with an area fraction of ~8.2%, consistent with the ferrite-stabilizing effect of very high Al and the simulated ferrite-free TA of ~1177 °C.
evidence_text: S3 exhibited extensive αp at 1050 °C (area fraction ∼14.6%), and αp persisted even at 1220 °C (area fraction: ∼8.2%), in line with the simulated requirement of ∼1177 °C for approaching a ferrite-free state and with the known ferrite-stabilizing effect of very high Al; a single experimental TA could therefore not be assigned to S3.

claim: S4 contained a visibly higher amount of polygonal ferrite at 1050 °C, but only a trace level (≲ 1%) at 1150 °C, consistent with its derivative-based TA of 1067 °C.
evidence_text: S4 contained a visibly higher amount of αp at 1050 °C, but only a trace level (≲ 1%) at 1150 °C, consistent with the derivative-based TA.

claim: S5 showed more prominent polygonal ferrite at 1050 °C and only a very small residual fraction (≲ 1%) at 1150 °C, while MatCalc simulations placed a carbide-dissolution temperature (Tcd) near ~775 °C and a ferrite-free TA around 986 °C.
evidence_text: S5 showed more prominent αp at 1050 °C and only a very small residual fraction (≲ 1%) at 1150 °C, while the simulations placed Tcd near ∼775 °C and a ferrite-free TA around 986 °C.

claim: Based on dilatometry, simulations, and LOM, an optimized austenitizing temperature of 1150 °C was applied for S1, S2, S4, and S5 to suppress polygonal ferrite and advance carbide dissolution, while 1220 °C was required for S3 to minimize αp, although a fully αp-free condition was not achieved.
evidence_text: Taken together, the three data streams (dilatometry, simulation, and LOM) point to 1150 °C as an optimized austenitizing temperature for S1, S2, S4, and S5, where carbide dissolution is advanced, chemical homogenization is improved, and αp is suppressed to negligible or non-existing levels. For S3, a higher schedule of 1220 °C was required to minimize αp and to approach the simulated ferrite-free field, although a fully αp-free condition could not be realized within the practical window.

claim: Measured martensite start (Ms) temperatures were 277 °C (S1), 274 °C (S2), 258 °C (S3), 305 °C (S4 after 1150 °C), 310 °C (S4 after 1250 °C), and 331 °C (S5), as shown in Fig. 5.
evidence_text: The measured values were 277 °C (S1), 274 °C (S2), 258 °C (S3), 310 °C (S4) after austenitizing at 1150 °C and 305 °C after austenitizing at 1250 °C, and 331 °C (S5) (see Fig. 5).

claim: The low Ms in S1–S3 indicates effectively carbon-enriched austenite; the lowest Ms in S3 (258 °C) is attributed to extended κ-carbide dissolution and carbon partitioning into austenite despite the Ms-raising tendency of high Al, while the high Ms in S5 (331 °C) reflects lower effective carbon content under the chosen condition.
evidence_text: In S1–S3, the relatively low Ms values indicate austenite that is effectively carbon-enriched after austenitization; the lowest Ms in S3 is consistent with extended κ-carbide (see Fig. 4 (c)) dissolution together with ferrite retention that facilitates local carbon partitioning into austenite despite the Ms-raising tendency of its very high Al content. In S5, the relatively high Ms (331 ◦C) reflects the lower effective carbon in austenite under the chosen condition

claim: Table 2 compiles the MatCalc-predicted TA, measured TA, applied TA, and measured Ms for steels S1–S5.
evidence_text: Table 2 compiles the MatCalc-predicted, the measured, the applied TA, and the measured Ms, forming the basis for the subsequent heat-treatment conditions.

claim: Table 2 compiles MatCalc-predicted, measured, and applied austenitizing temperatures (TA) and measured martensite start (Ms) temperatures, forming the basis for subsequent heat-treatment conditions.
evidence_text: Table 2 compiles the MatCalc-predicted, the measured, the applied T_A, and the measured Ms, forming the basis for the subsequent heat-treatment conditions.

claim: For steels S1 (Ms 277 °C) and S2 (Ms 274 °C), austempered at 350 °C, the bainitic transformation completed in approximately 1600 s for S1 and 2300 s for S2, with incubation times of 100 s and 210 s, respectively.
evidence_text: The steels S1 and S2 exhibit a minor difference of approximately 0.1 % in C-content, resulting in a slight variation in Ms temperatures, with S1 at 277 °C and S2 at 274 °C. During austempering at 350 °C, bainitic transformation was completed in approximately 1600 s for S1 and 2300 s for S2. The corresponding incubation times were 100 s for S1 and 210 s for S2.

claim: When austempered at 300 °C, S1 completed bainitic transformation in ~4000 s with an 80 s incubation, while S2 completed in ~3600 s with a 130 s incubation, showing faster kinetics for S2 at this lower temperature.
evidence_text: At a lower austempering temperature of 300 °C, the transformation durations were measured as 4000 s for S1 and 3600 s for S2. Under this condition, the incubation times were determined as 80 s for S1 and 130 s for S2.

claim: S3 (Ms 258 °C, TA 1220 °C) exhibited bainitic transformation completion at 350 °C in ~14,000 s with a 140 s incubation, and at 270 °C in ~43,000 s with a 120 s incubation; a slow bainite growth period of ~3000 s at 270 °C and ~1500 s at 350 °C was observed, and the 8.08 wt% Al did not accelerate bainite formation under these conditions.
evidence_text: For the S3 steel, Ms was determined as 258 °C at an austenitizing temperature of 1220 °C. As a result, during austempering at 350 °C, with a 140 s incubation period, the bainitic transformation reached completion at approximately 14,000 s. In contrast, at 270 °C with a 120 s incubation time, the transformation duration extended to approximately 43,000 s. A distinctive feature of the S3 steel is the presence of a slow bainite growth period following the initial incubation phase, lasting nearly 3000 s at TB of 270 °C and 1500 s at TB of 350 °C.

claim: S4 exhibited Ms of 305 °C (TA 1150 °C) and 310 °C (TA 1250 °C), with incubation times of 50–60 s across all conditions. Complete bainitic transformation times were 3000 s (TA 1150 °C, TB 350 °C), 3500 s (TB 320 °C), and 2600 s (TA 1250 °C).
evidence_text: S4 steel exhibited Ms of 305 °C at 1150 °C and 310 °C at 1250 °C. Among the investigated steels, S4, except for S5, exhibited the shortest and the roughly same incubation time, with 50–60 s across all parameters. The complete bainitic transformation times for S4 were determined as 3000 s at 1150 °C austenitization and 350 °C austempering, 3500 s at 320 °C, and 2600 s at 1250 °C.

claim: S5 (Ms 331 °C, 0.60 wt% C, 3.92 wt% Al) exhibited incubation within 10 s and complete bainite transformation in 700 s at 335 °C and 1000 s at 365 °C.
evidence_text: S5 steel, characterized by an Ms of 331 °C, contains relatively low C and Al contents (0.60 wt.% C and 3.92 wt.% Al, respectively). The incubation period was completed within 10 s, followed by a rapid bainitic transformation. Complete bainitic transformation was observed at 335 °C after 700 s and at 365 °C after 1000 s during austempering.

claim: In S5 steel (0.60 wt.% C, 3.92 wt.% Al, Ms 331 °C), the incubation period was completed within 10 s, and complete bainitic transformation occurred in 700 s at 335 °C and 1000 s at 365 °C during austempering.
evidence_text: S5 steel, characterized by an Ms of 331 ◦C, contains relatively low C and Al contents (0.60 wt.% C and 3.92 wt.% Al, respectively). The incubation period was completed within 10 s, followed by a rapid bainitic transformation. Complete bainitic transformation was observed at 335 ◦C after 700 s and at 365 ◦C after 1000 s during austempering.

claim: The relatively lower carbon concentration (0.60 wt.%) in S5 contributes to the acceleration of bainitic transformation by reducing austenite stabilization, consistent with the observation of shorter transformation times compared to higher-carbon alloys.
evidence_text: In addition to Al content, the relatively lower C concentration (0.60 wt.%) in S5 also contributes to the acceleration of the bainitic transformation. Carbon is known to stabilize austenite and delay the transformation, whereas its reduction facilitates faster transformation of bainite. This is consistent with the observed shorter transformation times in S5 compared to other alloys with higher carbon levels.

claim: In S5 steel, the presence of 0.78 wt.% Ni did not adversely affect the bainitic transformation kinetics, and the polygonal ferrite (αp) content was maintained at a minimal level due to the austenite-stabilizing effect of Ni.
evidence_text: Additionally, the presence of 0.78 wt.% Ni did not adversely affect the bainitic transformation kinetics. Similar to the S4 steel, the αp content was maintained at a minimal level due to the austenite-stabilizing effect of Ni, without any detrimental impact on the transformation behavior.

claim: The maximum yield strength (Rp0.2) was 1225 MPa for sample S1 at TB of 300 °C, and the minimum was 945 MPa for sample S3 at TB of 270 °C.
evidence_text: The maximum Rp0.2 values were recorded as 1225 MPa for sample S1 at TB of 300 ◦C and minimum as 945 MPa for sample S3 at TB of 270 ◦C.

claim: Upon austempering at 300 °C, S1 achieved UTS of approximately 1600 MPa with elongation 6.8% (PSE 10,880 MPa·%), and S2 achieved UTS 1590 MPa with elongation 7.0% (PSE 11,130 MPa·%).
evidence_text: Upon austempering at 300 ◦C, both alloys achieved high UTS values, approximately 1600 MPa for S1 and 1590 MPa for S2, with El of 6.8 % and 7.0 %, respectively. These correspond to PSE (product of strength and elongation) values of 10,880 MPa⋅% and 11,130 MPa⋅%.

claim: Increasing TB to 350 °C reduced the UTS to nearly 1380 MPa for both S1 and S2, while elongation improved to 8.7% for S1 and 9.4% for S2, resulting in increased PSE values of ~12,000 and 12,970 MPa·% respectively.
evidence_text: Increasing TB to 350 ◦C reduced the UTS to nearly 1380 MPa for both steels, while the El improved to 8.7 % for S1 and 9.4 % for S2, resulting in increased PSE values of ~12,000 and 12,970 MPa⋅%, respectively.

claim: At 270 °C slightly above Ms, S3 steel exhibited UTS of 1450 MPa but elongation dropped to 2.4%, resulting in a low PSE of approximately 3480 MPa·%, confirming a significant ductility-strength trade-off.
evidence_text: However, at 270 ◦C, slightly above the Ms temperature, ductility dropped to 2.4 % despite an increase in UTS to 1450 MPa, resulting in a low PSE of only ~3480 MPa⋅%, confirming a significant ductility-strength trade-off.

claim: Austenitizing S4 at 1150 °C and austempering at 320 °C gave UTS 1360 MPa, elongation 10%, and PSE 13,600 MPa·%; at 350 °C it gave UTS 1470 MPa, elongation 9.1%, and PSE 13,380 MPa·%.
evidence_text: Alternatively, austenitizing at 1150 ◦C resulted in UTS values of 1360 MPa and 1470 MPa with El of 10 % and 9.1 % at 320 ◦C and 350 ◦C, leading to higher PSE values of 13,600 and 13,380 MPa⋅%, respectively.

claim: The UTS–El performance of S5 steel is positioned intermediate between steels containing 3 wt.% Al (UTS 1400–2000 MPa, El 9–12%, PSE 12,600–24,000 MPa·%) and 5 wt.% Al (UTS 1300–1850 MPa, El 4–9.5%, PSE 5,200–17,575 MPa·%).
evidence_text: In the study of [15], alloys containing ≈3 wt.% Al exhibited UTS values between 1400 and 2000 MPa and El ranging from 9 % to 12 %, corresponding to PSE values in the range of approximately 12,600 to 24,000 MPa⋅%. In contrast, steels with ≈5 wt.% Al showed UTS values between 1300 and 1850 MPa and El values of 4 %–9.5 %, resulting in PSE values of roughly 5200 to 17,575 MPa⋅%. In this context, the UTS–El performance of the S5 steel can be positioned intermediate between steels containing 3 wt.% and 5 wt.% Al, and possessing comparatively higher C contents (ranging from 0.67 wt.% to 0.94 wt.%).

claim: In S1, S2, and S3 steels, bainitic transformation at higher TB (350 °C) resulted in significant retained austenite volume fraction (Vγ) reaching up to 34%, compared to 27–29% at lower TB temperatures.
evidence_text: In steels S1, S2, and S3, bainitic transformation at higher TB (350 ◦C) resulted in significant Vγ, reaching up to 34 %, which exhibits a more pronounced incomplete character compared to those obtained at lower TB temperatures, with Vγ ranging between 27 % and 29 %.

claim: In S4 and S5 steels with lower C–Al alloying levels, the effect of TB on retained austenite volume fraction (Vγ) was more limited, remaining below 30%.
evidence_text: In contrast, in the steels S4 and S5, which were developed with lower C–Al alloying levels, the effect of TB was more limited on the Vγ, remaining below 30 %.

claim: Average bainitic ferrite plate thickness Lt(t) ranged from 34 to 41 nm at low TB and from 55 to 68 nm at high TB across the studied steels.
evidence_text: average Lt(t) values range from 34 to 41 nm at low TB and 55–68 nm at high T overall, represented this correlation.

claim: In S3 at 270 °C, the phase-resolved micro-strain differed markedly between retained austenite (εγ: 0.54%) and bainitic ferrite (εα: 0.33%), consistent with pronounced strain partitioning.
evidence_text: In S3 at 270 ◦C, the phase-resolved micro-strain differed markedly between γr and bainitic ferrite (εγ: 0.54 % vs. εα: 0.33 %), consistent with pronounced strain partitioning.

claim: Pronounced strain partitioning in S3 at 270 °C promotes early localization and limits ductility, resulting in average elongation as low as 2.4% in tensile testing.
evidence_text: Such an imbalance promotes early localization and γ to bainitic ferrite transformation, thereby limiting ductility; accordingly, the average elongation decreased to as low as 2.4 % in tensile testing.

claim: Fig. 7 summarizes the mechanical properties (UTS, Rp0.2, elongation, and hardness HV30) of the investigated steels under various austenitization (TA) and austempering (TB) conditions.
evidence_text: Mechanical test results of the investigated parameters are given in Fig. 7 and critical points are discussed. Fig. 7. Mechanical properties of the investigated steels under various austenitization (TA) and austempering (TB) conditions. UTS (MPa), Rp0.2 (MPa), El (%), and hardness (HV30) are presented.

claim: Austempering temperature TB primarily tunes the UTS–El trade-off via bainitic plate thickness and retained-austenite stability.
evidence_text: TB primarily tunes the UTS–El trade-off via plate thickness and retained-austenite stability

claim: For S1 and S4 steels austempered at TB = 350 °C, the micro-strain in bainitic ferrite (εα) was notably higher, ranging between 0.30 and 0.45%, indicating a clearer heterogeneous dislocation distribution.
evidence_text: for S1 and S4 steels at TB of 350 ◦C, the εα was notably higher, ranging between 0.30 and 0.45 %. It showed that the degree of heterogeneous dislocation distribution was clearer at these parameters.

claim: In S3 austempered at 270 °C, the phase-resolved micro-strain differed markedly between γr and bainitic ferrite (εγ: 0.54% vs εα: 0.33%), with pronounced strain partitioning, which promoted early localization and limited ductility to an average elongation of 2.4%.
evidence_text: In S3 at 270 °C, the phase-resolved micro-strain differed markedly between γr and bainitic ferrite (εγ: 0.54 % vs. εα: 0.33 %), consistent with pronounced strain partitioning. Under this condition, the heterogeneity of the dislocation distribution became more pronounced. Such an imbalance promotes early localization and γ to bainitic ferrite transformation, thereby limiting ductility; accordingly, the average elongation decreased to as low as 2.4 % in tensile testing (see Table 3 for ε values).

claim: In S1 and S3 steels, (Fe,M)xCy particles precipitate during cooling due to extremely high nucleation kinetics between approximately 700 °C and 1150 °C; in S5, precipitation occurs between 700 °C and 1000 °C.
evidence_text: (Fe,M)xCy particles precipitate during cooling due to their extremely high nucleation kinetics between approximately 700 ◦C and 1150 ◦C in S1 and S3, and between 700 ◦C and 1000 ◦C in S5.

claim: In S4, initial nucleation of (Fe,M)xCy occurs rapidly between 700 °C and 950 °C, but is subsequently suppressed due to the counter-coarsening effect of the Ti–Mo–C precipitate system.
evidence_text: for S4, initial nucleation occurs rapidly between 700 ◦C and 950 ◦C, but is subsequently suppressed due to the counter-coarsening effect of the Ti–Mo–C precipitate system.

claim: A slight increase in (Fe,M)xCy precipitates in S3 compared to S1 and S2, attributed to higher Al content, causes a minor stoichiometric shift in the TTP curve nose with negligible impact on mechanical properties.
evidence_text: TTP curves also indicated a slight increase in (Fe,M)xCy in S3 compared to S1 and S2. However, this minor stoichiometric shift in the TTP curve nose, attributed to the higher Al content, appears to have a negligible impact on the mechanical properties.

claim: The spherical, cementite-like (Fe,M)xCy precipitates in S1, S2, and S3 showed no micro-void formation and had negligible effect on mechanical properties.
evidence_text: it can be claimed that accelerated nucleation of and spherical nature of these cementite-like (Fe,M)xCy precipitates showed no micro-void formation (see Fig. 9), hence evaluated as having negligible effect on the mechanical properties.

claim: EDX analysis revealed Si within the (Fe,M)xCy precipitates, interpreted as entrapment due to rapid cooling, which suppresses the precipitates; Mo presence may enhance precipitate stability.
evidence_text: The presence of Si within the Fe3C crystal, as revealed by EDX analysis see Fig. 9 (j), can be interpreted as the result of entrapment within the lattice due to rapid cooling, since under equilibrium conditions, Si is not expected to dissolve in Fe3C, hence have suppressing effect on them. Furthermore, the existence of Mo in these precipitates can have enhanced their stability, thereby inhibiting void formation.

claim: The observed particles exhibit a rounded morphology inconsistent with spinodal decomposition, indicating a nucleation-driven formation mechanism, and their composition is consistent with cementite-like carbides modified by Si and Mo.
evidence_text: The observed particles exhibit a rounded morphology inconsistent with spinodal decomposition, indicating a nucleation-driven formation mechanism. Given the apparent absence of significant Al within the particles, their composition is more consistent with cementite-like (Fe,M)xCy carbides modified by the presence of M = Si, Mo.

claim: E21-type stoichiometric perovskite κ-carbide was not observed microscopically; the limited Mn content (≈1 wt%) is insufficient to stabilize such phases, and remaining κ-carbides are presumed to exist as nanoscale, embedded, non-stoichiometric L12-type structures.
evidence_text: E21-type stoichiometric perovskite κ-carbide was not directly examined microscopically in this study. The absence of observable κ-carbide structures is attributed to the limited Mn content (≈1 wt.%), which is insufficient to stabilize such phases. As a result, no plate-like morphology was detected, and the remaining κ-carbides are presumed to exist in the matrix as nanoscale, embedded, non-stoichiometric L12- type structures.

claim: At TB = 350 °C, the standard deviation of bainitic ferrite plate thickness (Lt(t)) increased, indicating a broader range of bainitic structures including nB Type 1, Type 2, and blocky γr regions, especially in S1, S2, and S3; this heterogeneity promotes interlocked bainitic morphologies.
evidence_text: At TB of 350 ◦C, the standard deviation Lt(t) increased, indicating the presence of bainitic structures with a broader range of thicknesses, such as nB Type 1 and Type 2, alongside blocky γr regions located between them (see Fig. 9). This structural phenomenon is particularly pronounced in the S1, S2, and S3 steels (see Table 3 for the SD difference between the temperatures of TB). This heterogeneity promotes the formation of interlocked bainitic morphologies consisting of different bainite types.

claim: In S3, an unavoidable 8.2% polygonal ferrite (αp) is present; C-partitioning from αp into adjacent austenite locally stabilizes the austenite and inhibits bainitic transformation, resulting in sluggish γr and requiring 43,200 s at 270 °C to achieve near-complete transformation.
evidence_text: For steel S3, a modified austenitization approach was adopted due to the unavoidable presence of αp (8.2 %). ... the low C solubility of pre-existing αp leads to significant C-partitioning into the adjacent austenite, resulting in C-enrichment ... This partitioning locally stabilizes the adjacent austenite, thereby inhibiting austenite–bainite transformation ... the bainite growth could not be propagated and stalled at many of these sites, resulting in transformation-free regions that required significantly longer holding times to achieve complete bainitic transformation. ... consequently, a prolonged duration of 43,200 s was required to achieve near-complete transformation (see Fig. 9 (i)).

claim: In S3 austempered at 270 °C, the bainitic plate thickness (Lt(t)) was reduced from 58 nm to 40 nm, contributing to increased micro-strain heterogeneity and a severe drop in elongation.
evidence_text: at 270 ◦C, where the Lt(t) is reduced from 58 nm to 40 nm, the higher average εγ ... compared to the ferritic phases ... These factors explain the severe drop in El at that parameter.

claim: In S3 steel with 8.2% polygonal ferrite, C-partitioning from pre-existing δ_p into adjacent austenite locally stabilizes austenite and inhibits bainite growth, leading to transformation-free regions; at 270 °C, transformation remained incomplete after 25,200 s, and a prolonged duration of 43,200 s was required to achieve near-complete transformation.
evidence_text: at 270 °C for 25,200 s (see LOM Fig. 9 (h)), where the transformation remained incomplete; consequently, a prolonged duration of 43,200 s was required to achieve near-complete transformation (see Fig. 9 (i)).

claim: The S4 Ti-microalloyed steel exhibited the best average elongation of 10% and maximum elongation of 12% at austenitization temperature 1150 °C and austempering temperature 350 °C, slightly higher than S1 and S2.
evidence_text: the S4 steel exhibited the best average and maximum El of 10 and 12 % at TA 1150 °C and TB: 350 °C, which are slightly higher than S1and S2

claim: In S4, TiMoC precipitates suppress the formation of (Fe,M)xCy and MoC precipitates, resulting in less presence of these phases compared to S1, S2, and S3.
evidence_text: (Fe,M)xCy and MoC precipitates were less present in the final microstructure compared to steels S1, S2 and S3, due to the suppresing effect of TiMoC.

claim: An austenitization temperature of 1250 °C was selected for S4 to partially suppress impingement associated with extremely small prior austenite grain sizes observed at 1150 °C, thereby facilitating faster phase transformations.
evidence_text: an austenitization temperature of 1250 °C was selected. This temperature was also specifically chosen because it suppressed partially the impingement associated with the extremely small prior austenite grain sizes observed at 1150 °C, thereby facilitating relatively faster phase transformations.

claim: For S5 steel austempered at 335 °C, XRD shows the highest micro-strain in austenite (εγ: 0.51%) slightly above ferrite (εα: 0.45%) and a low retained-austenite fraction of 19%, yielding a UTS of 1530 MPa and elongation of 7%.
evidence_text: XRD at this condition indicates the highest micro-strain in austenite (εγ: 0.51 %), slightly above ferrite (εα: 0.45 %), and a lower retained-austenite fraction (19 %) (Table 3). These features explain the higher strength but reduced ductility measured at 335 °C (UTS: 1530 MPa, El: 7 %).

claim: At TB 365 °C, S5 exhibits coarser bainitic packets, a trace of allotriomorphic ferrite, no martensite, retained-austenite fraction increasing to ~26%, micro-strains converging (εγ: 0.42%, εα: 0.43%), UTS of 1315 MPa, and elongation of 8.7%.
evidence_text: The retainedaustenite fraction increases to ~26 % and the phase micro-strains converge (εγ: 0.42 %, εα: 0.43 %), consistent with reduced lattice curvature at the higher transformation temperature and improved deformation uniformity. The mechanical response follows accordingly (UTS: 1315 MPa, El: 8.7 %).

claim: KAM maps for S5 at 335 °C show mean KAM ~0.825° and median 0.692°, while at 365 °C mean KAM ~0.905° and median 0.789°, but the maps were acquired at different magnifications (25k× vs 20k×), so small differences should be interpreted with measurement sensitivity in mind.
evidence_text: the mean KAM ~ 0.905° vs 0.825°; median 0.789° vs 0.692°, whereas local KAM maxima are more pronounced near M at 335 °C. KAM is step-size sensitive; the maps were acquired at different magnifications (25k× vs 20k×).

claim: Dilatometry gave an Ms temperature of 331 °C for S5; sporadic lenticular martensite observed at 335 °C (Ms+4 K) is attributed to stress-assisted transformation and local compositional/thermal heterogeneities.
evidence_text: Although dilatometry gave Ms of 331 °C, the sporadic M plates at 335 °C (Ms+4 K) are compatible with stress-assisted transformation and local thermal/compositional heterogeneities

claim: S5 shows no SEM-resolvable (Fe,M)xCy precipitates after austempering, consistent with the TTP assessment and lower alloying level.
evidence_text: S5 shows no SEM-resolvable (Fe,M)xCy after austempering, in line with the TTP assessment and the lower alloying level
